g.text: row_count | int64 | total rows in table
page_count | int | leaf pages — table scan cost
ndv | int64 | distinct values per column
null_count | int64 | NULL rows per column

div.p: QUERY PLAN SEARCH USING INDEX idx_age (est 2 000 rows, sel=0.0200, cost=8 013.0) -- or -- SCAN TABLE users (est 950 000 rows, cost=14 000.0)

g.text: RANDOM_IO_FACTOR | 4.0 | random vs sequential I/O penalty
ROWS_PER_IDX_LEAF | 200 | index entries per leaf page
INDEX_FANOUT | 340 | keys per internal index page
MAX_INDEX_SELECTIVITY | 0.20 | force table scan above this

div.p: At sel > 20 % random double-lookups cost more than a sequential pass. Independence-assumption failures also dominate in this high-selectivity regime.
Emit: OpenIndexRead → SeekIdx → IdxRowid → SeekRowid → Column Double-lookup: index leaf → rowid → table page
Emit: OpenRead → Rewind → Column → ResultRow → Next Single sequential pass over all pages
Access: Full leaf-page traversal Always available — no index required Cost driver: page_count
Access: B+tree descent → leaf chain One plan per matching index on WHERE col Cost driver: tree_height + random lookups
Index wins decisively. est_rows × RANDOM_IO_FACTOR ≪ page_count e.g. sel=0.0001 → cost_idx≈44 vs cost_scan=14 000
Hardware-dependent break-even. sel_break = page_count / (row_count × RANDOM_IO_FACTOR) Tune RANDOM_IO_FACTOR per storage class.
Table scan wins. Random double-lookups dominate. e.g. sel=0.95 → 950 K random reads MAX_INDEX_SELECTIVITY cap = 0.20
